questions.question: Can Visitors create domains or vignettes?
questions.recommendation: No — treat Visitors as strictly read-only on all content
questions.rationale: Visitor role purpose is observation/reporting only, not content authoring

questions.question: How are Visitor accounts created?
questions.recommendation: Admins create them via a new user management UI in settings
questions.rationale: No self-registration needed; Visitor accounts are granted by admins only

assumptions: Visitor role is read-only: cannot create domains, vignettes, definitions, or API keys; cannot start runs/batches; cannot import data
Visitors can: view all reports/analysis, export data (CSV/Excel), change their own password
Two roles: Admin (full access) and Visitor (restricted). No intermediate tier.
Admin user management UI needed: admins can create, list, and edit users with role assignment
Archive tab, Settings tab except account password, and Domains Manage are hidden and URL-blocked for Visitors
Role enforcement is server-side at JWT and GraphQL resolver level, not just UI

answers.How are Visitor accounts created?: Admins create them via a new user management UI with role assignment.

non_goals: Fine-grained per-resource permissions beyond Admin/Visitor binary
acceptance_criteria: Admins can create, list, and assign roles to users via a management UI
Archive, Settings non-account, and Domains Manage tabs are hidden and URL-blocked for Visitors

parallel_analysis.note: Slices run serially (1→2→3→4→5). Within Slice 4, the requireAdmin additions to individual mutation files are independent of each other, but the factory dispatches them as a single slice. No cross-slice parallelism is safe.

judge_verdicts.reasoning: spec.codex.feasibility-adversarial.review#high-1 is not addressed. The current text still treats mutation writes as admin-only while also preserving visitor password self-service, and it never names a visitor write exception for account/password updates, so this remains unresolved and is not specific enough to implement. spec.codex.risk-adversarial.review#high-1 is not addressed. The spec does not define a first-admin bootstrap path after a fresh reset; the only reset note is the empty UI state, which does not explain how the first admin account is created. spec.gemini.requirements-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed in spec.md FR-009b, which requires first-login password reset behavior for new users; that mitigation is specific enough to implement.

evidence.quote: All GraphQL mutations MUST call `requireAdmin()`.
Change own password
`createUser` GraphQL mutation MUST allow ADMIN users
Empty-state message if no users exist
`mustChangePassword: true`

judge_verdicts.reasoning: There are no earlier findings or orchestrator responses, so none of the latest findings can be classified as restatements. The latest round is surfacing real new issues, including visitor password-change authorization, admin bootstrap deadlock, admin read API authorization, brittle route policy, export leakage, admin-set password impersonation, last-admin self-demotion, inconsistent password complexity, role-promotion latency, and access-matrix ambiguity. Since 0% are restatements, the loop is still producing signal.

evidence.quote: No prior findings yet.
FR-006 says all GraphQL mutations must call requireAdmin(), but the background, access matrix, and Story 3 say visitors must still be able to use /settings/account and change their own password.
The spec has no bootstrap or recovery path for the first admin account.
The spec does not define a bootstrap path for the first admin account after a fresh DB reset or brand-new deployment.
Server-side authorization is fully specified for mutations and POST /api/import, but not for admin-only read APIs.
Route protection is defined as a manual list of paths. That is brittle.
FR-008 says visitors can export evaluation data, but it only names a few things to exclude.
The createUser flow (FR-010) allows an admin to set a password for a new user. There is no requirement for the user to change this password on first login.
The spec does not prevent the last remaining admin from changing their own role to VISITOR via updateUserRole.
If the existing change password functionality does not enforce the same rule, a user could immediately change their strong, admin-set password to a weak one.
when a VISITOR is promoted to ADMIN, they will be unable to access admin functions until they log out and log back in
The Access Matrix entry for View Settings (all) uses the label Account only for the VISITOR role.

judge_verdicts.reasoning: Four load-bearing gaps would cause a competent implementer to guess or ship a bug. (1) lastLoginAt is required by listUsers (FR-012) but is absent from the User model additions in Key Entities — if the field does not already exist in the current schema, the migration is incomplete and the query crashes; the spec never says whether it already exists. (2) mustChangePassword enforcement is underspecified: FR-009b mandates a redirect 'before accessing any other page' but does not say where the intercept lives (client route guard, server middleware, or both), what event clears the flag, or whether the API itself blocks requests from mustChangePassword users — an implementer must invent all three decisions. (3) The last-admin atomic check requires protection against concurrent races, but the spec says only 'within a DB transaction' without specifying isolation level — Prisma default interactive transactions do not guarantee serializable isolation, leaving the race condition the spec explicitly prohibits. (4) API key role enforcement says 'fetched from DB at auth time' but does not identify which middleware or auth layer handles API key requests, making the insertion point ambiguous and easy to miss.

evidence.quote: FR-012: A new listUsers GraphQL query MUST return all users with: id, email, name, role, lastLoginAt, createdAt. [...] User model additions: role: UserRole @default(ADMIN), mustChangePassword: Boolean @default(false)

On first login, the user MUST be redirected to /settings/account with a prompt to set a new password before accessing any other page. This preserves admin control over account creation without requiring an email flow, while restoring non-repudiation.

updateUserRole MUST check-and-update atomically (within a DB transaction) that at least one ADMIN remains after the change, regardless of whose role is being changed (including self-demotion). Concurrent role changes that race to remove the last Admin must fail the transaction, not succeed silently.

evidence.section: Edge Cases — API key auth with Visitor role
evidence.quote: API key authentication MUST also include the user's role (fetched from DB at auth time, since API keys don't carry a payload). Restricted endpoints must reject API key requests from VISITOR users the same as JWT requests.

judge_verdicts.reasoning: spec.codex.feasibility-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed in SPEC FR-005c, which explicitly carves out `changePassword` as the VISITOR write exception, and the story text keeps `/settings/account` accessible for password updates. This is specific enough to implement. spec.codex.risk-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed in SPEC FR-009c with an explicit bootstrap path via the existing CLI `create-user` command and `--role` defaulting to `ADMIN`; this is specific enough to implement. spec.gemini.requirements-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed in SPEC FR-009b with `mustChangePassword` on new users, JWT flag propagation, a top-level redirect to `/settings/account`, and server-side clearing on `changePassword`; this is specific enough to implement. No HIGH findings remain unaddressed.

evidence.quote: Visitor write exceptions — the following mutations are explicitly allowed for VISITOR users and MUST NOT call `requireAdmin()`: `changePassword` (own password only).
This is the bootstrap path for the very first admin account on a fresh deployment or after a DB reset — it operates outside the role check system.
New users created via `createUser` MUST be flagged as requiring a password change on first login (`mustChangePassword: true` on the User record).

judge_verdicts.reasoning: All latest findings are NEW because the earlier rounds section says there were no prior findings. There are no earlier concerns or orchestrator responses to compare against, so none can qualify as restatements. The loop is still producing real signal and should block.

evidence.quote: No prior findings yet.
FR-006 says all GraphQL mutations must call requireAdmin(), but the background, access matrix, and Story 3 say visitors must still be able to use /settings/account and change their own password.
The spec has no bootstrap or recovery path for the first admin account.
The spec does not define a bootstrap path for the first admin account after a fresh DB reset or brand-new deployment.
Server-side authorization is fully specified for mutations and POST /api/import, but not for admin-only read APIs.
Route protection is defined as a manual list of paths. That is brittle.
FR-008 says visitors can export evaluation data, but it only names a few things to exclude. It does not define a strict allowlist of exportable fields or a schema contract.
The createUser flow (FR-010) allows an admin to set a password for a new user. There is no requirement for the user to change this password on first login.
The spec does not prevent the last remaining admin from changing their own role to VISITOR via updateUserRole.
If the existing change password functionality does not enforce the same rule, a user could immediately change their strong, admin-set password to a weak one.
when a VISITOR is promoted to ADMIN, they will be unable to access admin functions until they log out and log back in
The Access Matrix entry for View Settings (all) uses the label Account only for the VISITOR role.

judge_verdicts.reasoning: Three genuine implementation snags, not style concerns. (1) The mustChangePassword flow has a JWT staleness loop baked in: the spec says the frontend checks the flag from the JWT, and the server clears it in the DB on changePassword — but no mechanism is specified to refresh the JWT after changePassword succeeds. The JWT still carries mustChangePassword:true, so the route guard fires again on every navigation, leaving the user stuck in a redirect loop to /settings/account forever (until logout). This will ship as a bug unless the implementer invents a solution the spec does not describe. (2) The changePassword mutation behavior when mustChangePassword:true is entirely unspecified: should it require the old password (which the admin-set user may or may not know, depending on how the admin communicated it) or bypass the old-password check? This is a load-bearing branch point with no answer in the spec. (3) The three new GraphQL operations — createUser, updateUserRole, listUsers — have no formal input type or return type definitions anywhere in the artifacts. createUser inputs can be inferred from FR-010 prose; updateUserRole inputs (userId? email? role enum value?) must be guessed; return types for all three are entirely absent. An implementer cannot write a schema without guessing.

evidence.quote: The JWT issued on login MUST include the `mustChangePassword` flag. The web app MUST check this flag in a top-level route guard: if `mustChangePassword` is true, redirect to `/settings/account` before any other navigation. The flag MUST be cleared server-side when `changePassword` succeeds for that user.
new users created via `createUser` MUST be flagged as requiring a password change on first login (`mustChangePassword: true` on the User record)

Visitor write exceptions — the following mutations are explicitly allowed for VISITOR users and MUST NOT call `requireAdmin()`: `changePassword` (own password only). All other mutations are admin-only per FR-005a.

createUser | Mutation | Creates a new user with role; Admin only
updateUserRole | Mutation | Changes a user's role; Admin only
listUsers | Query | Returns all users; Admin only

judge_verdicts.reasoning: spec.codex.feasibility-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed in the spec by an explicit visitor write exception: FR-005c says `changePassword` is allowed for VISITOR users and must not call `requireAdmin()`, and the access matrix also allows visitors to change their own password. spec.codex.risk-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed by FR-009c, which names the CLI bootstrap path for the first admin account after fresh deploy or DB reset and says it operates outside the role check system. spec.gemini.requirements-adversarial.review#high-1 is addressed by FR-009b, which requires `mustChangePassword: true` for new users, top-level redirect to `/settings/account`, and returning a fresh JWT only after password change. Each mitigation is specific enough to implement.

evidence.quote: Visitor write exceptions — the following mutations are explicitly allowed for VISITOR users and MUST NOT call `requireAdmin()`: `changePassword` (own password only). All other mutations are admin-only per FR-005a.

The existing CLI script at `cloud/apps/api/src/cli/` (the `create-user` command) MUST be updated to accept a `--role` argument defaulting to `ADMIN`. This is the bootstrap path for the very first admin account on a fresh deployment or after a DB reset — it operates outside the role check system.

New users created via `createUser` MUST be flagged as requiring a password change on first login (`mustChangePassword: true` on the User record). The JWT issued on login MUST include the `mustChangePassword` flag. The web app MUST check this flag in a top-level route guard: if `mustChangePassword` is true, redirect to `/settings/account` before any other navigation.

judge_verdicts.reasoning: All latest findings are NEW because the earlier rounds section says there were no prior findings and no orchestrator responses. With zero prior addressed concerns, none of the latest findings can qualify as RESTATEMENT under the stated rule. The latest round is still producing signal, including visitor password-change authorization conflict, first-admin bootstrap deadlock, missing admin-read API authorization, brittle route protection, export allowlist gaps, admin-set password impersonation, last-admin self-demotion, inconsistent password complexity, promotion latency UX, and access matrix ambiguity.

evidence.quote: No prior findings yet.
FR-006 says all GraphQL mutations must call requireAdmin(), but the background, access matrix, and Story 3 say visitors must still be able to use /settings/account and change their own password.
The spec does not define a bootstrap path for the first admin account after a fresh DB reset or brand-new deployment.
The createUser flow (FR-010) allows an admin to set a password for a new user. There is no requirement for the user to change this password on first login.

judge_verdicts.reasoning: Four load-bearing ambiguities would cause an implementer to guess or ship a bug. (1) The changePassword mutation's new return type is never specified — the spec mandates a fresh JWT in the response but never defines the GraphQL return shape, leaving the implementer to invent a schema change and matching client code without guidance. (2) The spec mandates SERIALIZABLE isolation for the last-admin transaction but Railway commonly uses PgBouncer in transaction-pooling mode, where SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL is silently dropped or errors — the spec does not acknowledge this and provides no fallback. (3) Two route guards produce conflicting redirects with no stated priority: mustChangePassword → /settings/account and VISITOR-on-restricted-path → /; a new VISITOR user with mustChangePassword=true hitting /archive would trigger both simultaneously with no defined winner, risking a redirect loop. (4) The spec states '24h (the JWT TTL)' as fact when describing the stale-role window, but never references the config value or env var that controls this — an implementer who sets a different TTL will ship subtly wrong documentation and possibly miscalibrated behavior.

evidence.quote: the server MUST return a fresh JWT with `mustChangePassword: false` in the response — this is the only mechanism to clear the redirect loop without forcing logout

evidence.section: Key Entities — New GraphQL operations (changePassword not listed)
evidence.quote: FR-005c Visitor write exceptions — the following mutations are explicitly allowed for VISITOR users and MUST NOT call `requireAdmin()`: `changePassword` (own password only)
within a Prisma `$transaction` with `SERIALIZABLE` isolation level
if `mustChangePassword` is true, redirect to `/settings/account` before any other navigation
The redirect target for unauthorized routes MUST be the home page (`/`)
a demoted Admin retains write access for up to 24h (the JWT TTL)

unresolved_concerns.reasoning: All latest findings are NEW because the earlier rounds section says there were no prior findings and no orchestrator responses. With zero prior addressed concerns, none of the latest findings can qualify as RESTATEMENT under the stated rule. The latest round is still producing signal, including visitor password-change authorization conflict, first-admin bootstrap deadlock, missing admin-read API authorization, brittle route protection, export allowlist gaps, admin-set password impersonation, last-admin self-demotion, inconsistent password complexity, promotion latency UX, and access matrix ambiguity.

Four load-bearing ambiguities would cause an implementer to guess or ship a bug. (1) The changePassword mutation's new return type is never specified — the spec mandates a fresh JWT in the response but never defines the GraphQL return shape, leaving the implementer to invent a schema change and matching client code without guidance. (2) The spec mandates SERIALIZABLE isolation for the last-admin transaction but Railway commonly uses PgBouncer in transaction-pooling mode, where SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL is silently dropped or errors — the spec does not acknowledge this and provides no fallback. (3) Two route guards produce conflicting redirects with no stated priority: mustChangePassword → /settings/account and VISITOR-on-restricted-path → /; a new VISITOR user with mustChangePassword=true hitting /archive would trigger both simultaneously with no defined winner, risking a redirect loop. (4) The spec states '24h (the JWT TTL)' as fact when describing the stale-role window, but never references the config value or env var that controls this — an implementer who sets a different TTL will ship subtly wrong documentation and possibly miscalibrated behavior.
